---
title: "One case study, three estimators: California's Proposition 99 in `mlsynth`"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
    fig-width: 7
    fig-height: 4.2
jupyter: python3
---

# The decision tree as the paper's spine

A practitioner who arrives with a panel and a causal question does not face one
synthetic-control estimator but several dozen, each making different assumptions
and each appropriate in a different corner of the design space. The contribution
of `mlsynth` is not only that it implements these methods behind a single
`config -> fit() -> results` contract, but that it encodes the expertise for
*choosing* among them. That expertise is laid out as a decision tree (Figure 1):
a short sequence of questions about the panel --- is the treatment already
assigned or still to be designed? is it endogenous in a way the controls cannot
absorb? how many units are treated? do persistent latent factors drive the
outcome? do the donors themselves absorb some of the treatment? --- each of
which routes the analyst to a specific estimator. Every branch of the tree is
grounded in the arguments its methods' own authors give for them; the full,
maintained version is the package's `choose` companion, of which Figure 1 is the
top-level map.

The remainder of this section makes the tree concrete by walking one familiar
panel down three of its branches. The case is Abadie, Diamond and Hainmueller's
study of California's Proposition 99, the 1988 ballot measure that raised the
state cigarette tax; the outcome is per-capita cigarette sales and the
intervention date is 1989. Three nodes of the tree send this single panel to
three different estimators:

1. With one treated unit (the tree's "how many treated?" gate, Q0.6) and no
   reason to distrust the donors, the analyst lands on standard synthetic
   control. We fit it with Abadie's full predictor specification, solved by the
   MSCMT nested-optimisation backend.
2. The latent-factor gate (Q1.4, "do persistent factors and noise drive the
   outcome?") routes to the low-rank family. We take its principal-component-
   regression member, which denoises the donor pool before fitting.
3. The SUTVA gate (Q1.1, "are your donors contaminated by the treatment?") routes
   to the spillover-aware family. We take Cao and Dowd's estimator, which
   reinstates the neighbouring states that the standard analysis hand-excludes
   and models the tax-driven sales that leak across state lines.

The point of the exercise is methodological rather than substantive: the same
outcome and the same intervention date, three different sets of assumptions, and
--- because the tree dispatches to estimators that share one interface --- three
analyses that differ by only a field or two in the configuration. Reading them
side by side is what turns a single estimate into an argument about how much of
that estimate depends on which assumption.

## Setup

```{python}
import pandas as pd

RAW = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jgreathouse9/mlsynth/refs/heads/main/basedata"

# Curated Abadie sample: California plus 38 control states, with the full
# predictor set (retail price, income, demographics, lagged sales).
cal = pd.read_csv(f"{RAW}/augmented_cali_long.csv")

# Predictors of Abadie's specification.
predictors = ["p_cig", "loginc", "pct15-24", "pc_beer", "smk_75", "smk_80", "smk_88"]

cal[["state", "year", "cigsale", "Proposition 99"] + predictors].head()
```

The outcome is `cigsale` (per-capita packs), treatment is the `Proposition 99`
indicator (California from 1989 on), and the panel runs 1970--2000.

## 1. Standard synthetic control (full covariate specification)

The textbook estimator takes the treated unit as given and chooses donor weights
so the synthetic California matches the real one on a set of pre-treatment
predictors --- here Abadie's retail price, log income, the share aged 15--24,
beer consumption, and cigarette sales in 1975, 1980 and 1988. Matching on
predictors (rather than outcomes alone) requires the nested
predictor-weight / donor-weight optimisation of the MSCMT package, exposed in
`mlsynth` as `backend="mscmt"`.

```{python}
from mlsynth import VanillaSC

scm = VanillaSC({
    "df": cal,
    "outcome": "cigsale", "treat": "Proposition 99",
    "unitid": "state", "time": "year",
    "backend": "mscmt",            # nested V (predictor) / W (donor) search
    "covariates": predictors,      # the full Abadie specification
    "inference": False,            # placebo inference available via inference="placebo"
    "display_graphs": False,
}).fit()

att_scm = scm.att
print(f"Standard SCM (mscmt)   ATT = {att_scm:+.2f} packs per capita")
```

The estimate is around nineteen fewer packs per capita per year over the
post-1988 period --- the headline Proposition 99 result.

## 2. Low-rank synthetic control (principal component regression)

Standard SCM matches the *observed* donor series, noise and all. If you believe
the outcomes are generated by a handful of latent factors --- a common
justification for synthetic control in the first place --- you can do better by
first recovering that low-rank signal and fitting the synthetic control to the
denoised donors. This is the principal-component-regression route, reached in
`mlsynth` through `CLUSTERSC` with `method="pcr"`. It is the same case study, but
now the estimator assumes (and exploits) factor structure.

```{python}
from mlsynth import CLUSTERSC

pcr = CLUSTERSC({
    "df": cal,
    "outcome": "cigsale", "treat": "Proposition 99",
    "unitid": "state", "time": "year",
    "method": "pcr",
    "clustering": False,
    "rank": 4,                     # keep the leading four singular components
    "display_graphs": False,
}).fit()

att_pcr = pcr.att
print(f"Low-rank SCM (pcr)     ATT = {att_pcr:+.2f} packs per capita")
```

The low-rank fit lands close to the standard estimate: on this panel the donor
pool is already clean enough that denoising mainly confirms the result, which is
exactly the reassurance the comparison is meant to provide.

```{python}
#| label: fig-curated
#| fig-cap: "Observed California against the standard and low-rank synthetic counterfactuals on the curated donor pool."
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

years = list(scm.time_series.time_periods)
observed = scm.time_series.observed_outcome
cf_scm = scm.time_series.counterfactual_outcome
cf_pcr = pcr.time_series.counterfactual_outcome

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(years, observed, color="black", lw=2, label="California (observed)")
ax.plot(years, cf_scm, color="C0", ls="--", label="Synthetic (standard, mscmt)")
ax.plot(years, cf_pcr, color="C1", ls=":", label="Synthetic (low-rank, pcr)")
ax.axvline(1989, color="grey", lw=1, ls="-")
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("packs per capita")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.show()
```

## 3. Spillover-aware synthetic control (Cao and Dowd)

Both estimates above rest on SUTVA: the controls are unaffected by California's
tax. Cao and Dowd argue this is exactly where the standard analysis is fragile.
When California raises its tax, sales migrate to neighbours such as Nevada --- and
because synthetic control actively loads on the donors most correlated with
California, it tends to put weight on precisely the contaminated states. The
canonical analysis sidesteps this by *hand-excluding* a dozen suspect states from
the donor pool. Cao and Dowd's method (`SPILLSYNTH` with `method="cd"`) instead
keeps every state and estimates the direct effect and the spillover jointly,
under a contextually motivated spillover structure.

```{python}
from mlsynth import SPILLSYNTH

# Full panel, including the states the curated analysis drops.
full = pd.read_csv(f"{RAW}/prop99_with_dc.csv")
full = full[(full["year"] >= 1970) & (full["year"] <= 2000)].copy()
full["treat"] = ((full["state"] == "California") & (full["year"] >= 1989)).astype(int)

# States Cao and Dowd flag as exposed to Proposition 99 spillover.
affected = [
    "Alaska", "Arizona", "District of Columbia", "Florida", "Hawaii",
    "Massachusetts", "Maryland", "Michigan", "New Jersey", "Nevada",
    "New York", "Oregon", "Washington",
]

spill = SPILLSYNTH({
    "df": full,
    "outcome": "cigsale", "treat": "treat",
    "unitid": "state", "time": "year",
    "method": "cd",
    "affected_units": affected,
    "display_graphs": False,
}).fit()

att_sp = spill.att          # spillover-corrected direct effect
att_naive = spill.att_scm   # ordinary SCM on the same full pool
print(f"Spillover-corrected    ATT = {att_sp:+.2f} packs per capita")
print(f"Naive SCM (full pool)  ATT = {att_naive:+.2f} packs per capita")
print(f"Spillover contamination    = {att_naive - att_sp:+.2f} packs per capita")
```

Two things are worth separating here. Reinstating the excluded states changes the
donor pool, which on its own moves the naive estimate. Holding that pool fixed,
the gap between the naive and spillover-corrected estimates isolates the
contamination: the bias an SCM incurs by treating leaky neighbours as clean
donors.

```{python}
#| label: fig-spillover
#| fig-cap: "Cao and Dowd per-year direct effect of Proposition 99 on California."
post_years = sorted(full.loc[full["treat"] == 1, "year"].unique())
direct_by_year = spill.cd.alpha[0, :]   # row 0 is the treated unit

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(post_years, direct_by_year, marker="o", color="C3")
ax.axhline(0, color="grey", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("direct ATT (packs per capita)")
plt.show()
```

## Putting it together

```{python}
summary = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        ["Standard SCM (mscmt)", "curated 38-donor pool", "linear factor model, SUTVA", att_scm],
        ["Low-rank SCM (pcr)",   "curated 38-donor pool", "low-rank factors, SUTVA",   att_pcr],
        ["Spillover SCM (cd)",   "full pool + spillover", "linear factors, no SUTVA",  att_sp],
    ],
    columns=["estimator", "donor pool", "key assumptions", "ATT"],
)
summary["ATT"] = summary["ATT"].map(lambda x: f"{x:+.2f}")
summary
```

Three nodes of the decision tree, three estimators, three sets of assumptions ---
reached not by rewriting the analysis each time but by answering the questions
the tree asks and changing a single field in the configuration. The standard and
low-rank fits agree, which tells us the curated result is not an artefact of
pre-treatment noise; the spillover-aware fit tells us how much of that result
depends on the SUTVA assumption the first two take on faith. This is the role the
decision tree plays throughout the paper. It is not a catalogue of methods but a
procedure for selecting among them: each gate sharpens the causal question, each
leaf is an estimator validated against its source, and the shared `fit()`
contract is what makes the leaves comparable. The case study above is one
traversal; the sections that follow enter the tree at other gates --- many
treated units, staggered adoption, and the experimental-design branch where the
treatment has not yet been assigned --- and the same logic carries through:
identify the question precisely, and the library names the estimator that answers
it.
```
